original_hypothesis.title: NFT Thresholds Become Immutable After ERC20 Management Stranding Non-Threshold Revenue
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Once a LiquidInfrastructureNFT is transferred to the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 contract, setThresholds requires onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId). The ERC20 contract is now the owner but exposes no function to call setThresholds on managed NFTs. The ERC20 also cannot grant ERC721 approvals. If the Cosmos module deposits a new token type to the NFT, it cannot be withdrawn because getThresholds returns the frozen list and withdrawFromManagedNFTs only withdraws threshold-listed tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: withdrawFromManagedNFTs calls getThresholds() to determine which ERC20s to withdraw. If a new revenue token accumulates on the NFT that isn't in the threshold list, it's permanently stranded on the NFT contract. The ERC20 has no mechanism to update thresholds or withdraw arbitrary tokens from managed NFTs.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deployment script sets thresholds before transferring NFT: `nft.setThresholds(erc20s, erc20s.map(() => 0))`. After transfer, no further threshold changes possible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setThresholds requires onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId); ERC20 contract is owner but has no function to call setThresholds

preconditions: NFT is managed by ERC20 contract (transferred and added via addManagedNFT)
New revenue token type needs to be added to thresholds
No external mechanism to call setThresholds on behalf of the ERC20 contract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getThresholds() returns frozen list; withdrawFromManagedNFTs only withdraws listed tokens
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack - architectural limitation that strands new token types
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Revenue in unlisted tokens permanently inaccessible

economic_questions: Can the owner release the NFT, update thresholds, and re-add it?
Would releasing and re-adding disrupt ongoing withdrawal cycles?
How likely is a new revenue token being deposited to existing NFTs?
validation_requirements: Verify setThresholds reverts when called by non-owner non-approved after NFT transfer
Test release-update-readd workflow for threshold changes

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Revenue in new token types stranded on NFTs

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All ERC20 holders miss revenue from non-threshold tokens
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All non-threshold token balances on managed NFTs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify ERC20 contract cannot call setThresholds or grant ERC721 approvals

validation_result.mechanism: missing management function stranding non-threshold tokens
validation_result.impact_summary: LiquidInfrastructureERC20 becomes owner of managed NFTs but exposes no function to call setThresholds or grant ERC721 approvals, permanently freezing threshold lists and stranding any new token types deposited to managed NFTs.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies a missing capability in LiquidInfrastructureERC20. When an NFT is transferred to the ERC20 contract and added via addManagedNFT, the ERC20 contract becomes the owner of AccountId. The NFT's setThresholds function requires onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId), meaning only the ERC20 contract (as owner) or an address it has approved could call it. However, the ERC20 contract has no function to (a) call setThresholds on a managed NFT, (b) call approve() on a managed NFT's ERC721 to grant approval to another address, or (c) call setApprovalForAll() on a managed NFT. This makes thresholds effectively immutable while the NFT is managed.

The withdrawal path in withdrawFromManagedNFTs explicitly fetches the token list from getThresholds() and only withdraws those tokens. Any new token type that accumulates on the NFT (e.g., deposited by the Cosmos x/microtx module or sent directly) that is not in the frozen threshold list will be permanently stranded until the workaround is applied.

A workaround exists: the owner can call releaseManagedNFT to transfer the NFT back, call setThresholds, transfer the NFT back to the ERC20, and call addManagedNFT again. However, this workaround is operationally risky due to the separate require(true) bug in releaseManagedNFT and potential nextWithdrawal index corruption if done mid-cycle.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at LiquidInfrastructureNFT.sol lines 108-122 is correct - setThresholds has the onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId) modifier. The ERC20 contract's entire interface was reviewed and confirmed to have no function that calls setThresholds, approve, or setApprovalForAll on managed NFTs. The withdrawFromManagedNFTs function at LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol exclusively uses getThresholds() to determine which tokens to withdraw.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Access controlled by onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId). Once the ERC20 contract owns the NFT, no external party can call this, and the ERC20 contract has no function to invoke it.
Only withdraws tokens returned by getThresholds(). Any token not in the threshold list is silently skipped and remains stranded on the NFT.
Missing management functions: the ERC20 contract becomes the owner of NFTs but provides no mechanism to call setThresholds, approve, or setApprovalForAll on managed NFTs.

calling_sequence: 2. For each NFT: calls withdrawFrom.getThresholds() to get (address[] withdrawERC20s, uint256[])
3. getThresholds() returns thresholdErc20s set during setThresholds - which is frozen since ERC20 is owner and has no function to call setThresholds
4. Calls withdrawFrom.withdrawBalancesTo(withdrawERC20s, address(this)) - only withdraws threshold-listed tokens
5. Any ERC20 balance on the NFT that is NOT in thresholdErc20s remains stranded on the NFT contract
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect originates from the architectural gap: LiquidInfrastructureERC20 takes ownership of NFTs (via transferFrom in addManagedNFT flow) but provides no proxy function for setThresholds. In LiquidInfrastructureNFT.sol:108, setThresholds requires onlyOwnerOrApproved(AccountId) via OwnableApprovableERC721.sol modifier. The ERC20 contract IS the owner but cannot execute arbitrary calls. In LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol:335-337, withdrawFromManagedNFTs fetches thresholds and only withdraws those specific tokens. New token types accumulating on the NFT (e.g. from Cosmos x/microtx or direct ERC20 transfers) have no path to be withdrawn.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Thresholds set before NFT transfer to ERC20
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script contract-deployer.ts confirms: `await nft.setThresholds(erc20s, erc20s.map(() => 0))` is called BEFORE `transferNftToErc20AndManage`. After transfer, the deployment script never calls setThresholds again, confirming thresholds are frozen post-transfer.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an active attack but an architectural limitation that results in permanent fund stranding. Scenario: (1) NFT thresholds set to [TokenA] with amount 0. (2) NFT transferred to ERC20 and added as managed. (3) External source (Cosmos module, direct transfer) deposits TokenB to the NFT. (4) withdrawFromManagedNFTs is called: getThresholds() returns [TokenA], so only TokenA is withdrawn. TokenB balance remains permanently stranded on the NFT. (5) No function on ERC20 can update thresholds to include TokenB. The only workaround is the release-update-readd flow which requires owner intervention and carries its own risks.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. Any ERC20 token that accumulates on a managed NFT but is not in the frozen threshold list is inaccessible through normal operations. This affects all ERC20 holders who would otherwise be entitled to distributions from that revenue. The workaround (release, update thresholds, re-add) exists but is operationally complex, disrupts the management cycle, and is error-prone due to the separate require(true) bug in releaseManagedNFT.
recommended_mitigations: Add a function to LiquidInfrastructureERC20 like: function setManagedNFTThresholds(address nftContract, address[] calldata newErc20s, uint256[] calldata newAmounts) public onlyOwner { LiquidInfrastructureNFT(nftContract).setThresholds(newErc20s, newAmounts); }

Alternatively, add a function to grant ERC721 approval on managed NFTs: function approveManagedNFTOperator(address nftContract, address operator) public onlyOwner { LiquidInfrastructureNFT(nftContract).approve(operator, LiquidInfrastructureNFT(nftContract).AccountId()); }
Consider allowing withdrawFromManagedNFTs to accept an arbitrary token list parameter instead of relying solely on getThresholds()
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureNFT with owner=deployer. 2. Set thresholds to [TokenA] with amount 0. 3. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20. 4. Transfer NFT to ERC20 contract and call addManagedNFT. 5. Deploy TokenB and send some to the NFT contract directly. 6. Call withdrawFromAllManagedNFTs. 7. Verify TokenB balance remains on NFT (not transferred to ERC20). 8. Attempt to call setThresholds from any EOA - verify it reverts. 9. Verify ERC20 contract has no function to call setThresholds.
uncertainties: The severity depends on how likely new token types are to appear on managed NFTs - in a Cosmos x/microtx environment, this may be common if the module adds new coin types
The release-readd workaround is viable but untested for edge cases like mid-withdrawal-cycle releases
additional_testing_needed: Test the full release-update-readd workflow to confirm it works without side effects
Test that TokenB sent directly to a managed NFT is indeed not withdrawn by withdrawFromManagedNFTs
Test the interaction between releaseManagedNFT and active withdrawal cycles (nextWithdrawal > 0)
